---
title: "1.3 Expressions and Output"
subtitle: "Part 2: Arithmetic and Compound Expressions"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Expressions and Operators

### One expression evaluates to one value

<span class="term">Arithmetic Expressions</span> (算术表达式) consist of numeric values, variables, and arithmetic operators that evaluate to a single numerical value.

For example, the expression `2 + 3` evaluates to `5`.

Java uses the standard mathematical operators for addition (`+`), subtraction (`-`), and division (`/`).

## Arithmetic Operators

### Java operator symbols

The multiplication operator is written as `*`, as it is in most programming languages.

You may have noticed that `+` was also used to combine `String` and other values into new `String`s. More on this later.

::: {.tight-list}
- `+` addition
- `-` subtraction
- `*` multiplication
- `/` division
- `%` remainder
:::

## Numeric Result Types

### `int` and `double`

Arithmetic expressions can be of type `int` or `double`.

An arithmetic expression consisting only of `int` values will evaluate to an `int` value.

An arithmetic expression that uses at least one `double` value will evaluate to a `double` value.

## Truncating Division

### Decimal part thrown away

When you are doing division with two integers, you will get an integer result and the decimal part of the result will be thrown away.

This is called <span class="term">truncating division</span> (截断除法).

If you want a `double` result, you should make at least one of the values in the expression a `double`, like `2.0`.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: lcop1`

Textbook prompt: Run the code below to see all the operators in action.

Do all of those operators do what you expected?

What about `2 / 3`? Isn't it surprising that it prints `0`?

Change the code to make it print the decimal part of the division too by making at least one of the numbers a `double`, like `2.0`.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: lcop1`

```java
public class Test1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println(2 + 3);
        System.out.println(2 - 3);
        System.out.println(2 * 3);
        System.out.println(2 / 3);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: lcop1`

Runestone expects the first three lines and a changed final division:

```text
5
-1
6
0.6666666666666666
```

The test message says: make sure you change `2/3` to `2.0/3`.

## Logic Error Context

### Math errors can cause runtime failures

Math errors sometimes lead to runtime errors in code.

The textbook example notes that when the Hubble Space Telescope was launched to space in 1990, a math coding error in a formula caused it to point in the wrong direction.

Thorough testing is the only way to make sure there are no logic errors that will cause runtime errors in your code.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: cm2in-runtime-error`

Textbook prompt: The following code is trying to convert centimeters to inches, but it has a math error.

Run the code to see that there are no error messages, but it simply does the wrong calculation.

Can you fix the logic error in the code? `1` inch = `2.54` cms.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: cm2in-runtime-error`

```java
public class CmToInches
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.print("100 centimeters in inches is: ");
        System.out.println(100 * 2.54);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: cm2in-runtime-error`

Runestone expects the output value to be:

```text
39.37007874015748
```

The code should contain the division operator `/`.

## Divide by Zero

### Integer division by zero

Another runtime error that is possible in math expressions is dividing by zero, which is undefined in mathematics.

An attempt to divide an integer by the integer `0` will result in an <span class="term">ArithmeticException</span> in Java.

Try it in one of the active codes to see what happens.

## Compound Expressions

### Multiple operators

Expressions can be combined into <span class="term">compound expressions</span> (复合表达式) with multiple operators.

When compound expressions are evaluated, <span class="term">operator precedence</span> (运算符优先级) rules are used, just like when we do math.

Multiplication `*`, division `/`, and remainder `%` are done before addition `+` and subtraction `-`.

## Parentheses

### Grouping first

Anything in parentheses is done first.

It does not hurt to put in extra parentheses if you are unsure as to what will be done first or just to make it more clear.

::: {.tight-list}
- parentheses first
- then `*`, `/`, `%`
- then `+`, `-`
:::

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: compound1`

Textbook prompt: In the example below, try to guess what it will print out and then run it to see if you are right.

Remember to consider <span class="term">operator precedence</span>.

How do the parentheses change the precedence?

## Code Window

### `activecode:: compound1`

```java
public class TestCompound
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println(2 + 3 * 2);
        System.out.println((2 + 3) * 2);
        System.out.println(2 + (3 * 2));
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: compound1`

Runestone expects this output from `main`:

```text
8
10
8
```

The first and third expressions match because `3 * 2` is evaluated before `2 +`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define an arithmetic expression as numeric values, variables, and operators that evaluate to one number
- identify Java arithmetic operators `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, and `%`
- explain that an all-`int` expression evaluates to an `int`
- explain <span class="term">truncating division</span> for `int / int`
- identify logic errors where code runs but calculates the wrong value
- apply precedence rules and parentheses in compound expressions
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 3

[Next: Remainder operator and Pay Calculator →](1-3-part-3-remainder-and-pay-calculator.html)
